to pass those premises to start with or you wouldn't be in the room. But I also know from time to time, you will cringe and just know that, you know, that's okay. So, your master list is on pages 19 and 20. You also have a card, a Goldenrod card in your bag that has the master list on it. So you can kind of keep that kind of to one side and help stay on track if you want to and not be flipping back and forth. So I'm going to start with something pretty elementary. So I'm on page 22. And the idea of the rapport bridge or the affinity bridge is an important idea because people make almost all their trust decisions which trust allows for abdication of choice and abdication of responsibility. So how many of you from somebody in your life periodically get papers you need to sign and they come to you with little yellow sticky arrows on them where you need to sign and you sign them and you put them in the envelope and send them back and you don't read the document. Raise your hands. Come on and you'll learn more looking around at them than you will at me. See, not at half a smarter group because I gave everybody credit for it. Harry, you're an attorney. How many people do you send those documents to with little yellow sticky arrows on them and you know they come back and they haven't read them? Why? Why would you do such a thing? Well because we trust that person. And of course we don't want to read the damn documents and we don't quote have time to read the damn documents. And in some cases we might not understand what we read if we read them which is even a greater argument for not signing them without reading them but nonetheless this is how you saw the hands. This is how we all behave with somebody. How many of you with more than one? Come on. So we abdicate authority. We abdicate. We don't delegate. We abdicate authority. We abdicate responsibility to people we trust. We do it all the time. How many of you get something done for you business or personal by vendor that you have not price shopped at least in a long time? Yeah, look. Why? Because you trust that person. You don't necessarily want the lowest price but you trust them to be charging you a fair and a reasonable price. And you have actually now abdicated to them. So it happens all the time. And one of the ways that it happens with most people is what this FBI agent calls a rapport bridge. I sometimes call it an affinity bridge and of course a little note you might make, multiple bridges are even better than one. So the Bernie Madoff case, one of the categories of people that he took a large sum of money from were rich Jewish people in New York because he's a rich Jewish person in New York. And they all support the same charities. They're all at the same events. I asked Joan Rivers if she lost any money to Bernie and she said no because I already took that class. I don't invest in anything. I don't understand how the money is being made. She said but everybody I know lost money to Bernie. And so these affinity bridges and these rapport bridges when we create them change the dynamic of how people respond to us. And so that is an underlying thing. If you make that happen with somebody immediately they're natural and they're habitual and they're logical defenses start to go down. And there's four basic ways we do it. One is with empathetic statements. So yeah. My page is 12. So I do this a lot and I do it in copy. I do it orally. I do it one to one. In conversational settings I like to get people to tell me about their pain so I can make empathetic statements back to them about their pain. Why because it builds an affinity bridge. If it's not conversational then you have to integrate it into what you do. The more you integrate it in what you do the more people are likely to abdicate to you. So page 12 in the Ruthless Management book the subhead. So there's this you've all seen it but there's this thing the conflicting agendas. So this is all the employees agenda. Yours is just as one on it how much profit can we produce today on their list that's number 15 it's down here at the bottom. All this other stuff on their minds when they come to work is taking care of the kids holding their marriage together planning for their vacation planning for the upcoming weekend planning for a night out with the girls or the guys getting the bills paid grocery shopping finding out who Sue slept with over the weekend who will win American Idol social relationships at the office who's a bitch who's my friend social activities at the office lunch football pool birthdays somehow getting to work on time so they're fired getting off work right on time or early if possible. Fourteen all the way down there is compliance with the program at 15 is how much profit can I produce for the company today. That's an empathetic statement right because everybody gets it the cartoon I think it's on 18 so I get letters about this cartoon but then who will sign all our paychecks and he's out you know I'm out on the ledge ready to jump with a gun to my head and they're all leaning out the window saying but Dan who will sign all our paychecks right. I get letters I blew it up I put it on my wall that's exactly so the minute that happens that person's defenses towards me weaken and their trust in me because I understand that you can't go up and it's easy to do you can train yourself to do it the more you understand who it is you're dealing with of course the better you can do it and you can't overdo it so if you go through a book like that 30 percent of it if you take a highlighter now and you go through looking for empathetic statements with the owner of a business and you yellow highlight every empathetic statement you can find you're going to find roughly 30 percent of the book is empathetic statements. Next affinity statements or symbols. So affinity matters it never should it's a stupid way to make a decision about anybody or anything but it matters enormously right. So if you are a Republican candidate for anything it's a good idea to wear the flag pin they notice if you're not wearing it if you're a Democrat you don't have to and in some settings it would be better if you didn't you know a hammer and sickle will do fine but but but but but if you happen to be a Republican running for dog catcher it's a good idea to wear the flag pin right and they notice if you're not if you you may recall Obama got some crap over this early in his presidency people noticed that he was not wearing an American flag pin and then he wore it for quite a while and I noticed the other day it's not on the lapel again so you know it's last year F.U. I'm I'm doing what I want which I understand by the way but very well but yeah they don't like me now just wait we're gonna have some real fun in the last six months I promise but so affinity statements I'm one of you somehow and affinity symbols matter advocacy statements are slightly different than empathetic statements so advocacy statements this is one of the things Trump does so well so advocacy statements are saying what they would love to say but mostly won't standing up for some group against a rather ethereal opponent so if you go through if you take three issues and you may pick any three you like of of the newslet so if you do it currently you have to do the Dan Kennedy pages in the middle of the no BS if you want to go back before that and just get a no BS letter it doesn't matter by the way I'm very consistent about this and again you take a highlighter pen and you go through and look for advocacy statements me standing up for somebody against a a critical view of somebody else you will find you'll find them in every issue you won't find that you won't find me on meeting them anyway and you will tend to find them done a lot if you want a current reference so if you go to your February issue if you want to make a note and go to final thoughts this month on page 16 it's a giant advocacy statement it's a giant Trump rant on behalf of the little understood and unappreciated entrepreneur which pretty much all entrepreneurs feel they are underappreciated and put upon and odd man out and so forth so the first time anybody ever like talked about this to me it was in a very elementary way and yet I've never forgotten it or not used it so on page 24 you have a thing one of those wonderful acronyms that speakers love to use in seminar called form and it's your template for conversation with somebody because these are the four things in order that most people not you not us we probably many of us would have this scrambled but most people this is what they are into and these are the things they talk about all the time the things they like to talk about first is family you have to you know you meet somebody in line at Panera and women do it more than guys but now you don't have to go very far before they're showing your pictures on the phone of their ugly kids you know and you have to like not recoil at the site you know try not to ask is that a boy or a girl is it human or is you see that's so so family then the second thing they'll talk about is their occupation if they're in business they're more likely but they'll talk about their occupation it's like one of the top questions right what do you do it's information people exchange with each other right and workers all use that language what do you do we kind of use the language of what what business are you in right but it's the same thing then they'll go to recreation so if I play golf you're gonna have a conversation about golf if they bowl you're gonna have a conversation about bowling if they bet they're good we're gonna have a conversation about what game you put team you bet on last night it's etc etc and then finally is some conversation about money now that may be in the context of politics it may be in the context of the economy it may be personal but those that's what people tend to talk about and it's tends to be the order in which they talk about it so this is what it's still taught by the way and it should be so mostly if you join a multi-level so if you join Amway Shackley Herbalife etc Mary Kay which obviously Nick is signed up for got a little thing on the side here good for him if you join any of them you'll be taught this has the conversation template for strangers and also you do it in this order in that context because you want to have built your affinity bridges before you get to trying to recruit somebody so you build the family affinity bridge you build the occupation affinity bridge and you build the recreation affinity bridge before you finally get to talking about money and the formula is always disclosure with some kind of declarative statement in response to them why five kids let me show you there you know oh well so we have I have none but my sister has a so some kind of to relate a related question a follow-up question and a follow-up question if there's positive response so if they want to talk about family you stay there for a while if they don't seem responsive to family you move on and you ask a question about occupation you will land with everybody in one of these in one of these it's where they'll want to be it's where they'll be comfortable engaging in conversation you can also use as I said second-party affinity right so if you don't match somebody somehow can be used to give you a match right what do you do I own a bakery really my grandfather had a bakery right I mean at worst really I love donuts I mean you know I mean there's there's a second-party affinity right that that gets you into this conversation now what if we're not in a conversation we're doing it somehow at a distance through media you have to imagine the conversation and you have to basically do both ends of it but it's the same formula so people will often say to me when I'm writing copy for clients it's like why are we talking about all this irrelevant stuff where I grew up how many brothers and sisters I had my boat why do I show cars bring the cars all it's because of that's right because you get to build rapport bridges and affinity bridges with that stuff right so I this wealth manager client is named Ted Oakley I think third day I'll show you something is um and so Ted focuses on people who built businesses and are selling them and are going to have 20 million or more in a bucket and when I got Ted it took me it was about noon on the consulting day when I got to him telling me his life story and it turns out you don't like telling it and he sort of and of course he thought it was irrelevant right and his life story is raised by grandparents outhouse no indoor plumbing started selling stuff door to door to pay for his own clothes while he went to high school first person in his family to ever go to college he's the exact guy who now has 20 million in a bucket who built it up he got started one dry cleaner then he opened the second that he's the Stanley's millionaire next door guy if you're familiar with that reference that's Ted's client and most of them are middle of the country not coast they're in the same town they started their business in in many cases they're living in the same house that they've had for 20 or 30 years most of them got the same wife they've had for 20 or 30 years if he doesn't help them that's likely to change now that they have 20 million in a bucket but but at the moment they got the same spouse they've had for 20 or 30 years they started selling shit door to door to make money in many cases they're the first person in their family to go to college she's not using this at all he's showing them pie charts and how we apportion the money and the XYZ formula that somehow tracks every time Buffett has indigestion and so he's got all this stuff right I'm going this is it this is this is what we want to talk about because this door is right down my list right builds a powerful affinity bridge powerful so these things really matter we often think they don't matter but they really matter and they matter to most people a lot more than they should so the the critical thinker is absolutely right so Ted is absolutely right when he thinks it shouldn't matter if you're gonna hand 20 million dollars over to somebody to manage it the fact that you and they were both raised by your grandparents and had to sell greeting cards to get money together to buy the suit you wore to the prom should have nothing to do with handing him 20 million dollars of your money to manage but it has everything to do with you handing him 20 million dollars be their negatives I happen to believe you have no negatives it's just how you use them and so disclosure can be a very powerful thing one of the if you pay attention to Trump and you should whether
